text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION — APPROXIMATELY 3 MONTHS ON SYSTEMIC THERAPY

CLINICAL INDICATION:
62-year-old female with known Stage IIIB right upper lobe pulmonary adenocarcinoma, EGFR exon 21 L858R-mutant. Patient has been on systemic therapy with osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally daily for approximately 3 months since initiation. This examination is obtained for restaging purposes to assess treatment response. Prior imaging reference: whole-body PET-CT obtained at initial staging, demonstrating a hypermetabolic 4.8 cm right upper lobe spiculated mass (SUVmax 14.2) with hypermetabolic ipsilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy at stations 4R and 7 (SUVmax 8.7 and 6.4, respectively). No distant metastatic disease was identified at baseline. Brain imaging (dedicated MRI with gadolinium) at initial staging was negative for intracranial metastases.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of nonionic iodinated intravenous contrast (Omnipaque 350) with portal venous phase acquisition through the abdomen and pelvis. Chest images were acquired in the portal venous phase at 1.25 mm slice thickness reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Lung windows, mediastinal windows, and bone windows were reviewed. Comparison is made to the prior whole-body PET-CT.

FINDINGS:

LUNGS AND AIRWAYS:
The right upper lobe primary mass is substantially smaller compared to the baseline PET-CT. The dominant spiculated mass centered in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe now measures approximately 2.6 cm in its longest axial dimension, reduced from 4.8 cm at baseline — a decrease of roughly 46% in longest diameter. The mass retains mild spiculation along its margins, though the degree of architectural distortion in the surrounding parenchyma appears less pronounced than on the prior study. There is no longer appreciable abutment of the right upper lobe bronchus, and the bronchial lumen appears patent. A small area of peripheral ground-glass attenuation along the medial aspect of the mass likely represents residual peritumoural inflammation or partial lepidic component; this is markedly reduced compared to baseline. No cavitation is identified within the mass.

No new pulmonary nodules or masses are identified in the right or left lung. The visualized airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally. No endobronchial lesion is apparent. No lobar or segmental atelectasis. No consolidation to suggest superimposed pneumonia or obstructive post-obstructive change. Mild bilateral basal dependent atelectatic changes are present, a nonspecific finding. The background lung parenchyma demonstrates mild bilateral emphysematous changes, most prominent in the upper lobes, consistent with the patient's known history of mild COPD (GOLD Stage I), unchanged from prior.

PLEURAL SPACES:
No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or pleural-based nodularity.

MEDIASTINUM AND HILAR STRUCTURES:
The right paratracheal lymph node at station 4R has decreased markedly in size, now measuring approximately 0.9 cm in short axis, compared to 1.8 cm at baseline (short-axis measurement on CT; this node was hypermetabolic on baseline PET). The node retains mild residual soft tissue prominence but no longer meets size criteria for pathologically enlarged lymphadenopathy by standard CT measurement thresholds.

The subcarinal lymph node at station 7 similarly demonstrates substantial interval decrease, now measuring approximately 0.7 cm in short axis, compared to 1.5 cm at the baseline study (SUVmax 6.4 on PET-CT at diagnosis). This node appears nearly normalized in size.

No new mediastinal lymphadenopathy identified at stations 2R, 2L, 4L, 5, 6, 8, or 9. No prevascular or retrotracheal adenopathy. The superior mediastinum is otherwise unremarkable. The trachea is midline. The carina has normal morphology.

The right and left hila are unremarkable. No hilar lymphadenopathy is identified on the left. No vascular engorgement.

HEART AND PERICARDIUM:
The cardiac silhouette is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The visualized coronary arteries are without obvious calcification. The aorta and great vessels are of normal caliber. No evidence of pulmonary arterial enlargement to suggest pulmonary hypertension.

CHEST WALL AND BONY THORAX:
No rib fractures. No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions to suggest skeletal metastases within the imaged thorax. The thoracic vertebral bodies demonstrate normal height and alignment. No destructive lesion identified.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without focal hepatic lesion. No hypervascular or hypovascular mass. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal vein is patent.

Gallbladder and Biliary Tract: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No gallstones or gallbladder wall thickening. The common bile duct is not dilated.

Pancreas: The pancreas is of normal size and signal/attenuation. No pancreatic mass or ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesion.

Adrenal Glands: Both adrenal glands are of normal morphology and size. The right adrenal gland measures up to 0.8 cm in width; the left adrenal gland is similarly unremarkable. No adrenal nodule or mass. No adrenal thickening. This finding is unchanged from the baseline PET-CT, which noted the adrenal glands as unremarkable.

Kidneys and Ureters: The right kidney measures 10.4 cm and the left kidney measures 10.8 cm in craniocaudal dimension. Normal cortical thickness bilaterally. No hydronephrosis. A small 4 mm hypodense focus in the right kidney is too small to characterize but statistically likely represents a simple cyst; no follow-up is recommended. No renal mass identified. No urinary calculus.

Abdominal Aorta and Vasculature: The abdominal aorta is of normal caliber throughout. No aneurysmal dilatation. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The inferior vena cava is patent and of normal caliber.

Bowel: The visualized bowel loops are unremarkable. No bowel obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air or fluid.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder is partially distended and unremarkable in wall thickness. No pelvic free fluid. The uterus appears atrophic, appropriate for the patient's age. No pelvic sidewall lymphadenopathy. No pelvic osseous lesion.

IMPRESSION:

1. Substantial interval decrease in size of the right upper lobe primary lung mass, now measuring approximately 2.6 cm (previously 4.8 cm at baseline PET-CT), representing a decrease of approximately 46% in longest diameter. The residual mass retains mild spiculation but is markedly smaller, consistent with a meaningful treatment response to ongoing osimertinib therapy.

2. Marked interval reduction in size of the right paratracheal (station 4R) and subcarinal (station 7) mediastinal lymph nodes, with the right paratracheal node now measuring approximately 0.9 cm and the subcarinal node approximately 0.7 cm in short axis — both substantially decreased from the prior hypermetabolic lymphadenopathy documented on the baseline PET-CT. These nodes are approaching or at the lower threshold of normal size.

3. No new pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidation identified bilaterally. No new sites of disease in the chest.

4. No pleural effusion.

5. No hepatic, adrenal, or other intra-abdominal or pelvic lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement. Findings in the abdomen and pelvis are stable and unremarkable.

6. Stable mild bilateral upper lobe emphysematous changes consistent with known COPD. No acute pulmonary infectious or inflammatory process.

Note: Brain imaging is not included in this examination. Prior dedicated brain MRI at initial staging was negative for intracranial metastases; continued surveillance per the treating team's clinical protocol is recommended.

Overall, the findings are consistent with a substantial and ongoing treatment response of the intrathoracic disease to systemic therapy, with both the primary right upper lobe mass and the previously involved mediastinal lymph nodes having decreased considerably in size compared to baseline. No new sites of disease are identified on this examination.
